stories.headline: European allies reject Trump's call to send warships to Strait of Hormuz
stories.body: European countries have refused to send warships to help reopen the Strait of Hormuz, despite threats from Donald Trump that NATO faces "a very bad future" if members fail to assist. Germany's defence minister said "this is not our war" and questioned what European frigates could accomplish that the US Navy cannot. France, Australia and Japan also declined. The UK said it would not be drawn into a wider war but is exploring sending autonomous mine-hunting drones. Trump claimed "numerous countries" had told him they were on their way but refused to name any. The strait, which carried about a fifth of global oil and gas before the conflict, has been effectively blocked by Iran for two weeks, sending oil prices soaring above $106 per barrel, up more than 40 percent since strikes began on February 28.

stories.headline: Israel launches ground operations in southern Lebanon as conflict widens
stories.body: Israel has begun what it calls "limited and targeted ground operations" against Hezbollah strongholds in southern Lebanon, with troops from its 91st Division now on the ground. Israeli media report that three divisions, amounting to thousands of troops, are operating in southern Lebanon with two more expected in coming days. Israel is reportedly aiming to seize the entire area south of the Litani River, about 30 kilometres from the border. More than one million people have now been displaced in Lebanon, according to authorities. Defence Minister Israel Katz warned that displaced Shia residents would not return to their homes until Israeli security is guaranteed. The ground campaign fuels fears of a prolonged occupation reminiscent of Israel's 18-year presence in southern Lebanon that ended in 2000.

stories.headline: Iran war economic fallout deepens as Sri Lanka introduces four-day week
stories.body: The economic impact of the Iran conflict is spreading across South Asia, with Sri Lanka introducing a four-day working week to preserve shrinking fuel reserves. The country has also begun fuel rationing, limiting motorists to 15 litres per week. Bangladesh has imposed scheduled power blackouts and early Ramadan holidays for universities. Pakistan has moved schools to online teaching and raised fuel prices. Oil prices have surged past $106 per barrel, up over 40 percent since the war began, while LNG prices have risen nearly 60 percent. QatarEnergy suspended LNG production after an Iranian drone attack, and analysts warn prices could reach $150 per barrel if the conflict continues. Iran's attacks on UAE port Fujairah and Dubai's airport have further rattled energy markets and disrupted global shipping.

stories.headline: French parties scramble for alliances ahead of local election runoffs
stories.body: Political parties in France are negotiating strategic alliances before the final round of local elections this Sunday, after strong showings by the far right and radical left in the first round. In Paris, the right-wing candidate Rachida Dati struck a deal with centrist Pierre-Yves Bournazel to try to wrest control from the left after 25 years. The far-right Reconquest party, led by Eric Zemmour's partner Sarah Knafo, scored a historic 10 percent in wealthy western Paris. Jean-Luc Melenchon's La France Insoumise also made gains, winning the mayoral race outright in Saint-Denis. The municipal elections are seen as a crucial test of political alliances before the 2027 presidential election.

stories.headline: Severe weather batters US with tornadoes, blizzards and record heat simultaneously
stories.body: Over half the United States is in the path of extreme weather, with a rare combination of tornadoes, blizzards, a polar vortex, a heat dome and an atmospheric river all striking at once. More than 3,600 flights were cancelled Monday and nearly 6,800 delayed, compounded by a partial government shutdown that has strained TSA staffing at airports for over a month. Tornadoes struck southwest Michigan, devastating homes, while blizzards dumped heavy snow across Minnesota, Wisconsin and the upper Midwest. Southern California experienced a winter heat wave with temperatures in the 90s. The National Weather Service warned of the potential for strong and long track tornadoes as storms barreled toward the East Coast.

stories.headline: Afghan asylum seeker who worked with US forces dies in ICE custody
stories.body: A 41-year-old Afghan man who served alongside US special forces for over a decade has died less than 24 hours after being detained by ICE agents in Richardson, Texas. Mohommad Nazeer Paktyawal was taken into custody on March 13 as he left to drop his children off at school. His family said he was a healthy man and they cannot understand how he died. ICE said he complained of shortness of breath during processing and was taken to hospital, where he was declared dead the following morning. The agency labelled him a "criminal illegal alien" citing prior arrests for food benefit fraud, though advocacy groups say he was never charged or convicted. AfghanEvac called his death "indicative of an abdication of the duty of care."

stories.headline: Appeals court allows Trump's third-country deportations to proceed
stories.body: A federal appeals court has ruled that the Trump administration may continue its controversial practice of deporting immigrants to third countries, at least temporarily. The decision comes as Trump's mass deportation agenda faces a shake-up at the Department of Homeland Security. The administration has been deporting migrants to countries other than their origin nations, with Eswatini confirming it has received deportees as part of a deal with the Trump administration. Meanwhile, immigration data has become increasingly difficult to find as the administration pushes forward with deportations, and a judge separately paused the termination of deportation protections for some Somali immigrants.

stories.headline: White House chief of staff Susie Wiles diagnosed with breast cancer
stories.body: Susie Wiles, Trump's White House chief of staff and one of the most powerful figures in the administration, has been diagnosed with breast cancer. Trump himself disclosed the diagnosis, claiming to have played a role in connecting her with medical specialists and saying he had reversed a House Republican's terminal diagnosis in an apparently unrelated claim. Wiles, who served as the architect of Trump's successful 2024 campaign, is expected to continue in her role while undergoing treatment. The announcement comes at a particularly intense moment for the administration, which is managing the ongoing Iran conflict and a partial government shutdown.

stories.headline: PP wins Castilla y Leon election but remains dependent on Vox
stories.body: The PP has won the Castilla y Leon regional election, extending its lead over the PSOE and beating poll expectations, but still falls short of an absolute majority and needs Vox to govern. Across the three recent regional elections in Extremadura, Aragon and Castilla y Leon, a clear rightward shift is evident: the combined PP-Vox vote share now exceeds 54 percent. Vox has grown in all three elections and is the only party to do so, reaching nearly 19 percent in Castilla y Leon. PP leader Feijoo demanded Vox stop making excuses and reach coalition agreements, while Vox leader Abascal insisted his party would govern in all three regions. Alvise Perez's Se Acabo la Fiesta party drew votes from Vox but failed to win seats, while Podemos suffered a significant setback.

stories.headline: Sanchez to approve emergency economic measures Friday in response to Iran war
stories.body: Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez has called an extraordinary cabinet meeting for Friday to approve a comprehensive response plan to the economic effects of the Middle East conflict. The package will include significant tax relief on energy, targeted support for the agricultural, transport and fertiliser sectors, and structural measures to accelerate electrification and reduce oil dependence. The announcement comes a day after the EU Council meeting in Brussels, which will focus on coordinating member states' responses to the conflict. Sanchez's coalition partners PSOE and Sumar are still negotiating the scope of the measures with their parliamentary allies, mindful that PP, Vox and Junts previously blocked a similar social shield package.

stories.headline: King Felipe VI acknowledges abuses during Spanish conquest of the Americas
stories.body: King Felipe VI has acknowledged that there was "much abuse and ethical controversy" during Spain's colonisation of the Americas, in a significant gesture of rapprochement toward Mexico. Speaking during a visit to an exhibition on indigenous Mexican women at the National Archaeological Museum in Madrid, the king said some aspects of the conquest "cannot make us feel proud" when viewed through modern values, though he cautioned against "excessive moral presentism." Mexico has long sought an apology from Spain for the conquest, with former president Lopez Obrador making the request formally by letter. The remarks follow similar statements by Foreign Minister Albares last October acknowledging "pain and injustice" toward indigenous peoples.

stories.headline: CIS poll: 78% of Spaniards believe nuclear war is possible
stories.body: A new CIS poll reveals that 78.4 percent of Spaniards believe a major conflict involving nuclear weapons is possible, while seven in ten think Spain needs well-equipped armed forces as a security guarantee. A majority of 52 percent say the prospect of a world war generates maximum fear, and 48.6 percent fear a civil war. Some 78 percent of respondents also perceive a deterioration in Spanish democracy, a view shared across the political spectrum including 70 percent of PSOE voters and 69 percent of Sumar voters. In voting intention, the PSOE leads at 20.3 percent, followed by PP at 14.3 percent and Vox at 13.7 percent.

stories.headline: Education strikes begin in Barcelona with week of rolling stoppages across Catalunya
stories.body: Teachers and education workers launched a week of rolling strikes across Catalunya on Monday, beginning with schools in the Barcelones and Baix Llobregat areas. The USTEC union claimed 70 percent participation, while the Catalan education department put the figure at 32 percent. Between 8,000 and 25,000 people marched from Placa Urquinaona to Placa Sant Jaume, and strikers blocked access roads to Barcelona from early morning. The unions rejected a recent salary agreement between the department and CCOO and UGT, demanding deeper class-size reductions and higher pay increases. The strikes will continue through Friday with stoppages in different territories each day, culminating in a Catalunya-wide strike and mass demonstration in Barcelona on Friday.

stories.headline: Barcelona reactivates low-emission zone after Rodalies disruptions end
stories.body: Barcelona's low-emission zone (ZBE) is back in force from Monday after being suspended since January 22 due to the Rodalies commuter rail crisis. Vehicles without environmental labels are once again banned from circulating within the Rondes area on weekdays from 7am to 8pm. Mayor Jaume Collboni signed the decree reinstating the restrictions after the Generalitat confirmed that the exceptional circumstances justifying the suspension had ended. However, free Rodalies and Regional rail services have been extended until March 31, as several lines still operate with significant delays and over 200 temporary speed restrictions across the network. Meanwhile, three months of single-track works begin on the R2 Sud line through the Garraf tunnels.

stories.headline: Collserola intensifies wild boar captures amid African swine fever outbreak
stories.body: Authorities are intensifying efforts to capture and kill hundreds of wild boars in the Collserola natural park after an outbreak of African swine fever near Barcelona. The Agents Rurals are leading the operation, using American-made Pig Brig net traps that can capture entire family groups, along with silenced night-time shooting and tranquiliser darts. Access to Collserola has been closed to the public, and fauna passages remain sealed to prevent the boars from dispersing. The Generalitat initially estimated around 500 boars in the area. The operation is expected to be slow, and hunting season ends on March 31, after which professional hunters will no longer participate without additional incentives from the government.

stories.headline: Barcelona launches first fully electric double-decker tourist buses
stories.body: Barcelona's public transport operator TMB is putting its first fully electric double-decker tourist buses into service on Tuesday. The initial batch of three vehicles from Galician manufacturer UNVI will be followed by the remaining units from an order of 18, with all expected to be running by summer. The 12-metre-long buses feature overnight charging, advanced safety systems including blind-spot detection and pedestrian alerts, and will replace ageing diesel vehicles. The full electrification of the 65-vehicle tourist bus fleet will eliminate 1,285 tonnes of CO2 emissions annually. TMB is also acquiring five electric minibuses for neighbourhood routes, though the rest of the minibus fleet will need synthetic fuels due to battery size limitations.

stories.headline: New Hospital del Mar opens with advanced surgical suites and robotic pharmacy
stories.body: Barcelona's Hospital del Mar has inaugurated major upgrades including four new high-complexity operating theatres, a heliport, a therapeutic garden, and what is described as the world's first robotic pharmacy service. The expansion is part of a broader modernisation of the seaside hospital, one of the city's key public health facilities, which aims to improve its capacity for complex surgical procedures and patient care. The project also reflects the hospital's role as a reference centre in the Barcelona health system.
